column3:
Lateral radiograph shows a very subtle stress fracture of the calcaneus, which could have easily been missed if not for adequate patient history. The best sign of calcaneal stress fracture is a sclerotic band perpendicular to the primary trabeculae of the posterior process.
Sagittal T1 MR in the same patient is windowed to better show the calcaneal fracture , which is far more extensive than suggested on radiograph. Note the extensive bone marrow edema in the majority of the calcaneus.
Coronal PD FS MR in the same patient is shown. The stress fracture is harder to see in this plane; however, the florid marrow edema should prompt a search for fracture in other planes. Subjacent muscle edema is a common ancillary finding in calcaneus stress fracture.
column5:
Calcaneal stress fracture

Caption-derived keyword findings:
very subtle
sclerotic band perpendicular to the primary trabeculae of the posterior process
extensive bone marrow edema in the majority of the calcaneus
florid marrow edema
subjacent muscle edema

The radiographic clue is subtle sclerosis perpendicular to calcaneal trabeculae, while MR shows that the injury is more extensive because marrow edema involves much of the calcaneus. The plane-dependent visibility of the fracture line matches the source warning that the fracture line may be missed unless the correct plane and resolution are used.

Differential:
Stress response
Traumatic fracture
Osteomyelitis
Inflammatory arthritis

  • Pain increases with activity and improves with rest; hindfoot pain may be vague/deep, metatarsal pain sharp/pinpoint, and hallux sesamoid pain occurs with running or toe dorsiflexion.

column3:
AP radiograph in a runner with foot pain shows focal periosteal reaction at 2nd MT neck , usually the earliest radiographic sign of stress fracture. No fracture line is visible. This subtle finding may only be visible on a single radiographic projection and must be actively sought in the appropriate clinical setting.
AP radiograph 6 weeks later in the same patient shows maturing periosteal reaction , but the fracture itself is still not visible.
column5:
Metatarsal stress fracture

Caption-derived keyword findings:
focal periosteal reaction at 2nd MT neck
earliest radiographic sign
No fracture line is visible
single radiographic projection
maturing periosteal reaction
fracture itself is still not visible

The key recognition move is that early metatarsal stress fracture may be radiographically occult except for focal periosteal reaction. Persistence of maturing periosteal reaction despite no visible fracture line reinforces that absence of a lucent line does not exclude the diagnosis.

column5:
Cuboid stress fracture

Caption-derived keyword findings:
band of feathery bone marrow edema
cuboid
parallel the anterior or posterior margin of the bone
extend toward the cuboid sulcus
rare

The cuboid diagnosis depends on the source-described band-like feathery marrow edema and typical orientation parallel to the anterior or posterior margin with extension toward the cuboid sulcus. The caption also flags rarity, preventing over-ranking unless the location and orientation fit.

Differential:
Stress response
Traumatic fracture
Osteomyelitis
Inflammatory arthritis
column21:
Stress Fracture, Ankle and Foot
Source basis: RadPrimer article recovered in this audit bundle. Core cross-check text/pages were not supplied in the source package, so corrected cards intentionally avoid Core-only claims.

Terminology
  • Stress fracture: chronic, repetitive injury that leads to incomplete or complete fracture.
  • Fatigue fracture: abnormal stress on normal bone.
  • Insufficiency fracture: normal stress on weak bone.
  • Stress injury: bone marrow edema or long-segment periosteal reaction without fracture line; it can progress to fracture.
  • Pathologic fracture: fracture due to underlying tumor or infection.
Imaging spine
  • Radiographs are insensitive early; focal periosteal reaction is often the earliest sign.
  • Dorsal soft-tissue swelling on lateral view may be the only clue to metatarsal stress fracture.
  • A sclerotic band perpendicular to the long axis of bone is the most reliable radiographic sign.
  • MRI shows a jagged, irregular low-signal fracture line with surrounding marrow edema; the line may be missed if the correct plane/high resolution is not used.
  • CT can show a sclerotic or lucent incomplete/complete line; it is more sensitive than radiographs but less sensitive than MRI.
  • Bone scan shows focal abnormal increased uptake in one or more foot bones, may take up to 48 hours, and pinhole collimation improves anatomic detail.
Distribution and risk
  • Common sites in the body of the article: posteromedial distal tibia, calcaneus, and 1st-4th metatarsals; stress fracture can occur in any foot/ankle bone.
  • Low-risk nonunion sites: posteromedial distal tibia, calcaneus, 1st-4th metatarsals.
  • High-risk nonunion sites: anterior distal tibial cortex, navicular, 5th metatarsal base, medial malleolus, hallux sesamoids, talus.
  • Navicular stress fracture may progress to osteonecrosis and is easily missed dorsally.
Differential pivots
  • Stress response has edema/periosteal reaction without a fracture line.
  • Traumatic fracture is separated mainly by history and may be complete or incomplete.
  • Osteomyelitis is favored by skin ulcer/sinus tract to bone, confluent rounded marrow edema, and soft-tissue inflammation +/- abscess.
  • Inflammatory arthritis has marrow edema centered at a joint with erosions usually visible on radiographs or MRI.
Clinical and management
  • Pain increases with activity and improves with rest; hindfoot pain may be vague/deep, metatarsal pain sharp/pinpoint, and hallux sesamoid pain occurs with running or toe dorsiflexion.
  • Low-risk fractures: activity modification and continued weightbearing. High-risk fractures: restricted weightbearing and possible surgery.

column3:
AP radiograph in a runner with foot pain shows focal periosteal reaction at 2nd MT neck , usually the earliest radiographic sign of stress fracture. No fracture line is visible. This subtle finding may only be visible on a single radiographic projection and must be actively sought in the appropriate clinical setting.
AP radiograph 6 weeks later in the same patient shows maturing periosteal reaction , but the fracture itself is still not visible.
column7: Radiographs of the foot. Based on the image, name 4 key differentials and give one best discriminator for each.
column9:
Stress fracture: focal periosteal reaction or sclerotic/lucent line; early fracture line may be absent.
Stress response: periosteal edema +/- marrow edema but no fracture line; radiographs may be normal.
Traumatic fracture: history is the key discriminator and the fracture may be complete or incomplete.
Osteomyelitis: skin ulcer or sinus tract to bone, confluent rounded marrow edema, and surrounding soft-tissue inflammation +/- abscess.

column3: Sagittal STIR MR shows a stress fracture of the 2nd MT . Bone marrow edema extends nearly entire length of metatarsal. In addition, there is florid soft tissue edema surrounding the fracture site. This appearance may be confused for infection or tumor. In these cases, patient history is very important.
column7: MRI of the foot. Based on the image, name 4 key differentials and give one best discriminator for each.
column9:
Dominant pattern differs from other cases because: this drill centers on extensive marrow and soft-tissue edema around a metatarsal fracture, a pattern the caption says may mimic infection or tumor.
Stress fracture: look for a source-supported low-signal fracture line or stress-centered periosteal/callus change.
Osteomyelitis: skin ulcer or sinus tract to bone with confluent rounded marrow edema and surrounding soft-tissue inflammation +/- abscess.
Neoplasm: considered because the caption states tumor can mimic this edema-heavy appearance; do not diagnose tumor unless a mass-like lesion or aggressive tumor features are present in the actual case.
Traumatic fracture: separated primarily by acute trauma history and a complete or incomplete fracture pattern.

column3: Axial PD FS MR shows an early navicular stress fracture . These almost always originate at the dorsal, proximal cortex of the navicular and then progress distally and to the plantar surface. Osteonecrosis may develop as a long-term complication.
column7: MRI of the midfoot. Based on the image, name 3 key differentials and give one best discriminator for each.
column9:
Dominant pattern differs from other cases because this drill centers on the navicular dorsal cortex and a high-risk midfoot location rather than generic metatarsal periosteal reaction.
Stress fracture: source-described fracture line with marrow edema; early navicular stress fracture almost always originates at the dorsal, proximal cortex and progresses distally/plantarly. Osteonecrosis is a possible long-term complication, not the primary differential label in this source.

Traumatic fracture: separated primarily by an acute trauma history and complete or incomplete fracture morphology.

column11: Why can repetitive loading progress from stress injury to a visible fracture line?
column12:
The source defines stress injury as bone marrow edema or long-segment periosteal reaction without a fracture line and states it can progress to fracture.
Mechanism framed from the source: repetitive loading first produces edema/periosteal repair response; with continued stress, the injury can organize into an incomplete or complete fracture line.
Imaging consequence: early radiographs may show only focal periosteal reaction, while MRI may show marrow edema before or around a low-signal fracture line.

column11: Why can a healing stress fracture appear as a sclerotic band on radiographs?
column12: The source states that a healing stress fracture often develops a thick sclerotic band due to callus. Mechanistically within the allowed source set, the fracture site undergoes repair: callus and sclerosis accumulate along the stressed fracture plane, so radiographs may show a sclerotic band rather than a conspicuous lucent gap. In the calcaneus, the source-described band is typically oriented perpendicular to the primary trabeculae, which explains why a subtle heel radiograph can still reveal the diagnosis when the band orientation is recognized.

column13: What early-radiograph pitfall can lead to a miss, and what safety check prevents it?
column14:
Trap: Calling the study negative because no fracture line is visible.
Safety check: Actively inspect for focal periosteal reaction and compare all projections, including the lateral view for dorsal soft-tissue swelling in metatarsal cases.
Key discriminator: The source states focal periosteal reaction is often the earliest radiographic sign and that dorsal soft-tissue swelling on lateral view may be the only clue to metatarsal stress fracture.
Why it happens: Early stress fracture may be radiographically insensitive, so the indirect repair/inflammatory clues appear before the fracture line.

column13: What search-pattern pitfall can cause an early navicular case to be missed, and what safety check prevents it?
column14:
Trap: Missing an early navicular stress fracture because the abnormality is small and dorsal.
Safety check: Search the most dorsal long-axis images through the navicular and the dorsal margin of the navicular on short-axis images.
Key discriminator: RadPrimer states early navicular stress fracture is easily missed and almost always originates at the dorsal, proximal cortex.
Why it happens: The early fracture begins dorsally and may be conspicuous only on the correct image plane/location, while the site is high risk for nonunion and osteonecrosis.

column13: What MRI pitfall occurs when edema is obvious but a line is not, and what safety check prevents it?
column14:
Trap: Stopping at marrow edema and failing to search for the fracture line.
Safety check: Review other planes and use the correct plane/high-resolution imaging when the edema pattern is suspicious.
Key discriminator: The source states the fracture line may not be visible if the correct plane and high resolution are not used, and a calcaneal case notes florid edema should prompt a search for fracture in other planes.
Why it happens: The low-signal fracture line can be plane-dependent, while marrow edema is more diffuse and easier to see.

  • MRI shows a jagged, irregular low-signal fracture line with surrounding marrow edema; the line may be missed if the correct plane/high resolution is not used.
  • CT can show a sclerotic or lucent incomplete/complete line; it is more sensitive than radiographs but less sensitive than MRI.
  • Bone scan shows focal abnormal increased uptake in one or more foot bones, may take up to 48 hours, and pinhole collimation improves anatomic detail.
Distribution and risk
  • Common sites in the body of the article: posteromedial distal tibia, calcaneus, and 1st-4th metatarsals; stress fracture can occur in any foot/ankle bone.
  • Low-risk nonunion sites: posteromedial distal tibia, calcaneus, 1st-4th metatarsals.
  • High-risk nonunion sites: anterior distal tibial cortex, navicular, 5th metatarsal base, medial malleolus, hallux sesamoids, talus.

  • Traumatic fracture is separated mainly by history and may be complete or incomplete.
  • Osteomyelitis is favored by skin ulcer/sinus tract to bone, confluent rounded marrow edema, and soft-tissue inflammation +/- abscess.
  • Inflammatory arthritis has marrow edema centered at a joint with erosions usually visible on radiographs or MRI.
Clinical and management
  • Pain increases with activity and improves with rest; hindfoot pain may be vague/deep, metatarsal pain sharp/pinpoint, and hallux sesamoid pain occurs with running or toe dorsiflexion.
  • Low-risk fractures: activity modification and continued weightbearing. High-risk fractures: restricted weightbearing and possible surgery.

column15: What imaging checklist helps avoid missing early navicular stress fracture?
column16:
Look on the most dorsal long-axis images through the navicular.
Also check the dorsal margin of the navicular on short-axis images.
Typical origin: dorsal, proximal cortex with progression distally and plantarly.
Exam pivot: Navicular is a high-risk site and may progress to osteonecrosis.

column15: How does the source distinguish stress response from stress fracture?
column16:
Stress response: periosteal edema +/- bone marrow edema without fracture line.
Stress fracture: fracture line is present, though it may be occult if the correct plane/high resolution is not used.
Metatarsal pivot: edema in a metatarsal should be treated as stress fracture regardless of whether the fracture line is seen.

column15: What findings favor osteomyelitis over ankle/foot stress fracture in the source differential?
column16:
Osteomyelitis clues: skin ulcer and sinus tract extending to bone.
Marrow edema: confluent and rounded.
Soft tissues: surrounding inflammation +/- abscess.
Exam pivot: Do not overcall infection from edema alone without the source-supported soft-tissue/ulcer pathway.

column15: What activity-specific ankle/foot stress fracture locations are source-stated?
column16:
Runners: posteromedial distal tibia, distal fibula, navicular.
Dancers: metatarsal shafts or bases, distal anterior tibia.
Jumpers: distal anterior tibia, calcaneus.
Exam pivot: Activity can help target the search pattern without replacing imaging diagnosis.

column4: What stress-fracture locations are highlighted by this foot graphic?

column15: How does the source define pathologic fracture in the stress-fracture differential framework?
column16:
Pathologic fracture: fracture due to underlying tumor or infection.
Use: this separates repetitive-loading stress injury from fracture driven by destructive disease.
Exam pivot: if imaging suggests tumor or infection, do not force the case into fatigue/insufficiency stress-fracture language.

column15: What pain patterns are source-stated for ankle/foot stress fractures by location?

General: pain increases with activity and improves with rest.
